story:
When Martin called from overseas, I braced for bad news. Instead, he raved about a tiny Bulgarian village called Boarden and a bed-and-breakfast named Mora’s Inn—peaceful, clean, unbelievable food—and promised he’d be back by month’s end. Then the month passed, and another, and my messages went unanswered. Certain something was wrong, I flew to Sofia, rode a bus into the green folds of the Balkans, and stepped into a village square ringed with centuries-old houses—one a library, one a store, and one a guest house that had to be Mora’s Inn.

Inside, an old woman with sharp features sat spinning yarn on a drop spindle. Martin, perched on a huge floor pillow with a book, looked up and grinned. We hugged, and he introduced me: “John, this is Mora, my landlady.” Mora nodded, her accent clipped and kind. “Welcome. You stay tonight?” I agreed, catching a fleeting shadow cross Martin’s face before he smiled again.

Over dinner, Mora said, “Eat. This food is very tasty. Martin knows.” Then she left us to talk. “Tomorrow at dawn you’ll kick in my door with a pistol and fire,” I said. “The shot won’t draw blood; it’ll only knock Mora senseless, and her young face will slide back into the old one. Martin stared. “What?” I finally asked what I’d crossed an ocean to ask. Why was he still here? He flared, then softened. “I should’ve called. But this is my life now. I took a job teaching English at the local school. I’m not going back.”

Past midnight, full of rakia and stories, I dropped into bed. At dawn, footsteps woke me. A young woman stood at my bedside—long brown hair, a gauzy nightdress, not Mora the elder. “I’m Mora’s granddaughter,” she said. “My name is Mora, too. I’m Martin’s girlfriend.” It figured—something was keeping him here. She sat, a single tear sliding down. “All I wanted was for someone to stay in this house, generation after generation, and me watching over it, as it has always been. But the house is empty now. People leave. One day Martin will, too. And he will die before me. You will, too.”

Her hand rested, warm and bold, on my thigh. I stood. “Whatever this is, Martin’s my best friend. You need to go.” The door flew open. Martin strode in, lifted a pistol, and fired. The crack shattered the morning quiet. Mora collapsed to the floor—no blood, no wound—stunned as if the sound itself had knocked her senseless.

“Look at her face,” Martin said, dropping the gun and a book onto the bed. Dreaded, I turned her over. The youthful features had slipped into the old woman’s: white hair, wrinkled brow—Mora. My laugh came out jagged. “Okay. You got me. Great prank.”

“This isn’t a prank. She’ll wake in a couple of hours. The shock breaks her glamour,” he said, tapping the book: Household Spirits and Creatures of Eastern Europe. “Read ‘Kikimora.’”

He explained: Mora is a kikimora, a household spirit tied to a home. The last owner moved away and returned only in summer, leaving her lonely—until Martin rented the place. 

“How long did it take you to realize what was going on, Martin?”

“Well, I’d been renting this place for a week before the owner left for the winter. I saw Mora the very first day and thought she was the owner’s mother. But nobody in the village knew Mora, and nobody called this house Mora’s Inn. I was getting uneasy about the whole situation, when one night, Mora brought in a bottle of rakia and sat down to dine with me. At the end of the night she turned into a beautiful young woman. I thought I had drunk too much, so I went to bed. But the following morning, the same beautiful woman appeared in my room with my coffee and breakfast.”

“I understand why you didn’t want to tell me all this over the phone, Martin.”

I flipped through the book. A chapter heading was circled: “Domovik — Household God and Husband of Kikimora.” “What do you have to do to become one of them?” I whispered.

Martin didn’t answer. He hesitated, turned to the door, and in the small lamp’s light his shoulders seemed slouched, his hair ashen—already, somehow, older.
